critique.summary: The article claims that Cloudflare has flagged the domain archive.today as associated with command and control (C&C) or botnet activity and that it no longer resolves via Cloudflare's 1.1.1.2 DNS service. However, the provided content does not contain any substantive information or evidence supporting these claims.
mainPoints: archive.today no longer resolves via Cloudflare's 1.1.1.2 DNS resolver.
critique.truthfulness: The article content is insufficient to verify the claims made in the title. The text shown is a Cloudflare security verification page, which does not provide any evidence or confirmation that archive.today is flagged as malicious or that it is blocked on 1.1.1.2. Independent verification from Cloudflare's official threat intelligence or DNS filtering lists is necessary to confirm these claims. Without such evidence, the claims remain unsubstantiated.
considerations: archive.today is a popular web archiving service often used for preserving web pages.
Cloudflare's 1.1.1.2 DNS service is designed to block malware and adult content, but blocking decisions are subject to change and should be verified with official sources.
False positives in DNS-based blocking can occur, so confirmation from multiple sources is important.
The article does not provide any sources or data to support the claim.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion revolves around Cloudflare's blocking of archive.today and related domains due to their classification as command and control (C&C) and botnet infrastructure, with some users providing context on the ongoing DDoS attacks and legal pressures. Opinions vary on the legitimacy of Cloudflare's actions, the impact on users, and the technical details behind the DNS blocking.

comments.author: winkelmann
comments.text:
"archive.today is currently categorized as: * CIPA Filter * Reference * Command and Control & Botnet * DNS Tunneling"

Ditto for their other domains like archive.is and archive.ph

Example DoH request:

$ curl -s "https://1.1.1.2/dns-query?name=archive.is&type=A" -H "accept: application/dns-json"

{"Status":0,"TC":false,"RD":true,"RA":true,"AD":false,"CD":false,"Question":[{"name":"archive.is","type":1}],"Answer":[{"name":"archive.is","type":1,"TTL":60,"data":"0.0.0.0"}],"Comment":["EDE(16): Censored"]}

---

Relevant HN discussions:

https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=46843805 "Archive.today is directing a DDoS attack against my blog"

https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=47092006 "Wikipedia deprecates Archive.today, starts removing archive links"

https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=46624740 "Ask HN: Weird archive.today behavior?" - Post about the script used to execute the denial-of-service attack

Wikipedia page on deprecating and replacing archive.today links:

https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wikipedia:Archive.today_guidan...
comments.analysis: This comment provides detailed evidence from Cloudflare's DNS response and links to relevant discussions, making it a well-supported and accurate contribution.

comments.author: jeremie_strand
comments.text: The DNS tuneling flag alongside C&C/botnet is the odd one — that category implies data exfiltration or firewall bypass, not just aggressive crawling or DDoS behavior. Would be interesting to know what traffic pattern triggered it.
comments.analysis: This comment insightfully questions the classification categories, highlighting technical nuances that suggest a deeper investigation is needed.

comments.author: rollulus
comments.text:
I think there are two angles to look at this. Yes, there’s the attack on the weblog. But there’s also pressure on archive.today, e.g. an FBI investigation [1] and some entity using fictitious CSAM allegations [2].

[1]: https://arstechnica.com/tech-policy/2025/11/fbi-subpoena-tri...

[2]: https://adguard-dns.io/en/blog/archive-today-adguard-dns-blo...
comments.analysis: This comment adds important context about legal and investigative pressures on archive.today, supported by external links, enhancing the overall understanding.

comments.author: _moof
comments.text: Good. You don't get to use my computer for a DDoS. I don't care why the DDoS was happening. I wasn't asked, and that's a serious breach of trust.
comments.analysis: Expresses a strong personal stance against unauthorized use of resources for attacks, reflecting a common user perspective but lacking technical detail.

comments.author: f-serif
comments.text:
A bit context if you are confused why Public DNS server blocking websites. 1.1.1.2 is Malware blocking DNS server similar to AdBlock DNS server. It is not 1.1.1.1 and 1.0.0.1

Here is the DDoS context https://gyrovague.com
comments.analysis: Clarifies the distinction between Cloudflare's DNS services, helping readers understand the specific service involved in blocking archive.today.

comments.author: stuffoverflow
comments.text: Archive.today's attack on https://gyrovague.com is still on-going btw. It started just over two months ago. Some IPs get through normally but for example finnish residential IPs get stuck on endless captchas. The JS snippet that starts spamming gyrovague appears after solving the first captcha.
comments.analysis: Provides direct observation of the alleged attack behavior, adding practical evidence to the discussion though without external verification.

comments.author: 1vuio0pswjnm7
comments.text:
Some time ago, probably at least a year, likely more, I read a blog post by someone working for Google in Europe who loved using Archive.today and out of curiosity tried to determine who was running it. In the end he gave up, offered to buy the operator a beer or something like that, but if I recall correctly he went to even greater lengths in his research than the blogger discussed in this thread

I wish I could find it
comments.analysis: Offers anecdotal context about the obscurity of archive.today's operator, which aligns with the general theme of mystery but is unverifiable here.

comments.author: acejam
comments.text:
It amazes me that people still use and recommend Cloudflare's DNS servers for resolution. Cloudflare DNS does not support EDNS Client Subnet. As a result, DNS queries resolved by their service are likely to return IP addresses for many CDNs that are physically farther away from you, leading to a slower internet browsing and viewing experience.

Sacrificing performance for a faster lookup time makes no sense in 2026. This is the one area where I continue to use Google DNS as it just works. Use anything but Cloudflare in this case, please.

Parent pro-tip: Next time the iPad is having Bluey episode playback issues, check to see if you're actually using Cloudflare DNS.
comments.analysis: Technically accurate critique of Cloudflare DNS's limitations affecting performance, relevant to users choosing DNS providers.

comments.author: breppp
comments.text: While I fully support this instance, I wonder what else Cloudflare has set to "Censored", apart for the obvious CSAM
comments.analysis: Raises a valid question about the scope of Cloudflare's censorship, though speculative without evidence.

comments.author: anonym29
comments.text: I, for one, completely trust Cloudflare on this one. The guys running a MiTM attack on a substantial chunk of all global internet traffic, and working tirelessly to ensure billions of people behind CGNAT in the global south can't access the free and open web are the premiere experts on malicious, predatory, harmful internet-scale network behavior, after all.
comments.analysis: This sarcastic comment expresses distrust of Cloudflare, adding emotional tone but lacking constructive or factual content.
